diff_check_reasoning: This problem can be solved in just a few lines of Python code using basic list slicing and the modulo operator (`k % len(nums)`) to handle cases where `k` is greater than the list length. It does not require complex data structures or advanced algorithms, and most students familiar with fundamental Python list operations will be able to solve it quickly.

title: Valid Parenthesis String
problem_statement: Given a string s containing only three types of characters: '(', ')' and '*', return true if s is valid. The following rules define a valid string: Any left parenthesis '(' must have a corresponding right parenthesis ')'. Any right parenthesis ')' must have a corresponding left parenthesis '('. Left parenthesis '(' must go before the corresponding right parenthesis ')'. '*' could be treated as a single right parenthesis ')' or a single left parenthesis '(' or an empty string ''.
diff_check_reasoning: This problem is a more complex variation of the classic "Valid Parentheses" problem. The addition of the '*' wildcard means a simple stack-based approach is no longer sufficient. Students must devise a more advanced strategy, such as using two stacks (one to track the indices of '(' and another for '*') or a greedy approach that tracks the minimum and maximum possible number of open parentheses. While it is solvable, it requires careful handling of edge cases, deeper algorithmic thinking, and more lines of code than a basic problem, making it a solid intermediate challenge that many, but not all, students will successfully complete.

problem_statement: The look-and-say sequence is generated by reading the previous term. The first term is '1'. title: Josephus Survivor
problem_statement: There are n people standing in a circle, numbered 1 to n. Starting from person 1, every k-th person is eliminated. The counting continues from the next person. Return the position (number) of the survivor.
diff_check_reasoning: This is a classic, well-known problem (Josephus problem) that can be solved with a straightforward simulation using a list and modulo arithmetic in just a few lines of code. The concept of circular counting and elimination is intuitive, and most students familiar with basic loops and modulo can implement a working solution quickly. While an optimized O(n) solution using the recursive formula J(n,k) = (J(n-1,k) + k) % n requires more insight, the naive simulation approach makes this an easy problem for the majority of students.

title: String to Integer (atoi)

Read in next characters until a non-digit character or end of input is reached. The rest of the string is ignored. 4. Convert these digits into an integer (i.e. '123' -> 123, '0032' -> 32). If no digits were read, the integer is 0. 5. If the integer is out of the 32-bit signed integer range [-2^31, 2^31 - 1], then clamp the integer so that it remains in the range. Specifically, integers less than -2^31 should be clamped to -2^31, and integers greater than 2^31 - 1 should be clamped to 2^31 - 1. 6. Return the integer as the final result. Note: Only the space character ' ' is considered a whitespace character. Do not ignore any characters other than the leading whitespace.
